lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Hold, take these keys, and fetch more spices, nurse.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: They call for dates and quinces in the pastry.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Come, stir, stir, stir! the second cock hath crow’d,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: The curfew-bell hath rung, ’tis three o’clock:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Look to the baked meats, good Angelica:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Spare not for cost.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Go, you cot-quean, go,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Get you to bed; faith, You’ll be sick to-morrow

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: For this night’s watching.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: No, not a whit: what! I have watch’d ere now

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: All night for lesser cause, and ne’er been sick.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Ay, you have been a mouse-hunt in your time;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: But I will watch you from such watching now.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: A jealous-hood, a jealous hood!

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVINGMAN
lines.text: Things for the cook, sir; but I know not what.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Make haste, make haste. Sirrah, fetch drier logs:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Call Peter, he will show thee where they are.

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVINGMAN
lines.text: I have a head, sir, that will find out logs,

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVINGMAN
lines.text: And never trouble Peter for the matter.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Mass, and well said; a merry whoreson, ha!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Thou shalt be logger-head. Good faith, ’tis day:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: The county will be here with music straight,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: For so he said he would: I hear him near.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Nurse! Wife! What, ho! What, nurse, I say!

stage_directions.text: Enter three or four Servingmen, with spits, logs, and baskets.
Go waken Juliet, go and trim her up; I’ll go and chat with Paris: hie, make haste, Make haste; the bridegroom he is come already: Make haste, I say. Exeunt.

glosses.definition: aromatic fruit used in jams and marmalade — wedding-feast spice
the kitchen where baking is done
evening bell-ringing that rang again at dawn
meat pies for the wedding breakfast
a man who meddles in household work — Lady Capulet teases her husband
chaser of women — Lady Capulet's sly jab at her husband's younger-day rakishness
jealousy personified in a hood — affectionate teasing

annotations.title: Capulet in his kitchen — comedy before catastrophe
annotations.body: The Capulets, up through the night preparing the wedding, bicker affectionately about quinces, baked meats, the cost of musicians. Capulet calls himself a 'cot-quean' (a man who meddles in housework); Lady Capulet accuses him of being a 'mouse-hunt' in his youth. The tone is domestic, comic, entirely quotidian — a narrative pause before the discovery of Juliet's 'corpse' three dozen lines later. The twenty-four-line scene functions as Shakespeare's last rest-beat: the everyday texture of family life, made visible so that its destruction a moment later registers its full weight. Directors often cut this scene; in the text, it performs a specific structural job that cutting eliminates.
sources: Susan Snyder, The Comic Matrix of Shakespeare's Tragedies (1979)

trials.prompt: What structural purpose does the short wedding-prep scene (4.4) serve?
options: A last rest-beat of quotidian family life — a tonal quiet before the discovery of Juliet's 'corpse' in 4.5
It provides comic relief at random
